text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 21 September 2025 

Dr Patel  
St. George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Emily Carter, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H392847 NHS No. 9045127731  
22 Willow Bank, Tooting, London SW17 8EP 

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification. Prior event during air travel (reported 2023). 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte as required for clusters 

Plan: 

- Continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily.  
- Clobazam 10 mg at night used sparingly for short periods during higher-risk weeks; patient advised to avoid daytime sedation when travelling.  
- Arrange repeat ambulatory EEG and MRI with epilepsy protocol given persisting episodes and historical in-flight event.  
- Seizure and trigger diary to continue; patient has agreed to record sleep, stress levels, and travel exposure.  
- Discussed aviation-related anxiety; offered referral to clinical psychology for travel anxiety management.  
- Safety advice reinforced (bathing supervision, heights, machinery, and adherence to DVLA guidance).  
- Follow-up in 4 months or sooner if escalation occurs. 

I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today. She reports a longstanding history of episodic events, with a notable previous seizure occurring during air travel; she describes significant anticipatory anxiety around flying since then. There is no clear precipitant beyond sleep disruption and travel-related stress, and there are no consistent focal warnings reported. 

Over the past year seizure control has been relatively stable. She experiences two generalised tonic-clonic seizures every 2 months. Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly, and myoclonic jerks are reported only occasionally in the mornings. She keeps a detailed diary which broadly supports this pattern, with clusters sometimes following long-haul flights or nights of poor sleep. 

She previously trialled Lamotrigine (titrated to 150 mg twice daily) but discontinued due to rash and dizziness. She also had a brief trial of Topiramate (up to 50 mg twice daily) which was stopped for cognitive slowing. She feels Levetiracetam has been better tolerated overall, with occasional irritability that she manages through exercise and structured routines. 

We discussed strategies to mitigate travel-related triggers including sleep prioritisation, hydration, avoiding alcohol, and using aisle seats to reduce stress. She expresses interest in graded exposure and psychological support to address her travel anxiety, which I believe is appropriate. 

I will update you following the planned investigations and our next review. Please feel free to contact me if there are any concerns in the interim. 

With best wishes,

entities.text: Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly

Over the past year seizure control has been relatively stable. She experiences two generalised tonic-clonic seizures every 2 months. Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly, and myoclonic jerks are reported only occasionally in the mornings

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies mentioned: The letter states: "She experiences two generalised tonic-clonic seizures every 2 months." This equates to 1 per month. It also states: "Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly," which implies up to 2 per week. Myoclonic jerks are "only occasionally in the mornings" without a clear numeric frequency, so not used. Per the rule to select the highest frequency across seizure types, 2 per week (absence seizures) is more frequent than 1 per month (GTC). Therefore, the final label is 2 per week. Provide a supporting quote from the letter.
seizure_frequency_number: Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly
reference: Over the past year seizure control has been relatively stable. She experiences two generalised tonic-clonic seizures every 2 months. Absence seizures remain infrequent, usually no more than twice weekly, and myoclonic jerks are reported only occasionally in the mornings
